name: Code Graph Model (CGM)
maintainer: Ant Group / CodeFuse AI team (Ant Group, ShanghaiTech University, Zhejiang University, Shanghai Jiaotong University); key correspondents: Hang Yu, Jianguo Li, Peng Di
license: [uncertain] — CodeFuse organization typically uses Apache 2.0 or MIT; CGM-specific LICENSE not confirmed from available sources
last_major_release: [uncertain] — NeurIPS 2025 paper; GitHub repo codefuse-ai/CodeFuse-CGM active as of mid-2025; no formal versioned release tag confirmed
activity: [uncertain] — repo created 2025; NeurIPS 2025 acceptance indicates active research; star count not confirmed from search results; Ant Group / CodeFuse broader ecosystem has 4K+ stars across projects
storage_backend: Filesystem / in-memory graph (code graph is constructed and held in memory or serialized to disk during preprocessing). No external database. The R4 RAG framework uses vector index for semantic search in the Retriever step — likely an in-process embedding index.
representation_primitive: Code-AST + entity+edge. Nodes represent typed code entities: REPO, PACKAGE, FILE, TEXTFILE, CLASS, FUNCTION, ATTRIBUTE. Hierarchical 'contains' edges derived from AST parsing form a tree mirroring the file system. Reference edges (calls, imports, extends, implements) derived from lightweight semantic analysis and may form cycles. Node content is encoded by a pretrained encoder (CodeT5+) into fixed-size node tokens via a learned Adapter, then injected into the LLM's attention mechanism — making this a hybrid code-AST + neural-token representation unique among the surveyed papers.
api_shape: Python library + research framework; no MCP server; no REST API. The R4 chain (Rewriter, Retriever, Reranker, Reader) is orchestrated as a Python pipeline. A CGM MCP server wrapper exists as a third-party community project (cgm-mcp on mcpservers.org) but is not first-party.
hosting: self-host only; GPU required for CGM model inference; no managed cloud offering
llm_dependency: both ingest and retrieval. Ingest: CodeT5+ encoder encodes node content into node tokens; Adapter is a trained neural component. Retrieval: Qwen2.5-72B-instruct drives Rewriter and Reranker steps; CGE-Large (open-source embedding model) drives semantic search in Retriever. Reader (the CGM LLM itself) is Qwen2.5-72B fine-tuned with graph-attention injection.
schema_extensibility: fixed — node types (REPO, PACKAGE, FILE, TEXTFILE, CLASS, FUNCTION, ATTRIBUTE) and edge types (contains, calls, imports, extends, implements) are hardcoded in the Code Graph Generator. No YAML configuration or LLM-driven schema discovery.
schema_richness: 7 node types: REPO, PACKAGE, FILE, TEXTFILE, CLASS, FUNCTION, ATTRIBUTE. 5 edge types: contains (hierarchical, from AST), calls, imports, extends, implements (all reference edges from semantic analysis). Multi-label: not described. Multi-graph: not described. This schema covers the code-structure sublayer well (7 entities, 5 edges) but has no concept of issues, PRs, decisions, or any non-code artifact. Substantially below Gimle §5's target of 20+ entities / 15+ edges when measured against cross-domain requirements.
temporal_model: none — snapshot-based; graph is constructed once per repository commit. No bi-temporal metadata, no event log. Reflects a fixed codebase state.
forgetting_policy: none — static snapshot; full rebuild on update
ingest_latency_class: batch-offline — full AST parsing + semantic analysis of the entire repository, followed by neural encoding of all node content via CodeT5+. GPU-accelerated but still minutes to hours for large repos.
retrieval_modes: hybrid: graph-traversal (heuristic subgraph extraction via anchor-node matching in Retriever) + vector/semantic (CGE-Large embedding similarity in Retriever). Reranker uses Qwen2.5-72B for LLM-based relevance scoring. The final context is a compact subgraph of the most relevant files.
retrieval_agenticity: multi-hop implicit (the graph structure encodes multi-hop relationships that are traversed during subgraph extraction) + policy-guided (Rewriter reformulates queries; Reranker filters). The R4 chain is a fixed 4-step pipeline rather than a dynamic agent, but its Rewriter/Reranker steps constitute policy-guided retrieval.
retrieval_budget_control: yes, partially — the Retriever narrows candidates to a fixed subgraph size; Reranker selects a configurable top-K files. No explicit token-budget parameter exposed as a first-class API.
multi_project_namespace: none — single-repository instantiation; no multi-project namespace support documented
multi_user_access: none — research framework; no access control
embedder_options: CodeT5+ (for node token encoding, built-in); CGE-Large (open-source, built-in for semantic retrieval in Retriever step). Custom embedder not documented. No OpenAI or Ollama adapter.
llm_client_options: Qwen2.5-72B-instruct (open-source, default for Rewriter and Reranker). CGM's Reader is a fine-tuned Qwen2.5-72B variant. No Anthropic/OpenAI client built-in. LLM cannot be disabled — the architecture fundamentally depends on neural components.
cost_privacy: Self-hosted inference; no data leaves the host. GPU cost is significant: Qwen2.5-72B requires multi-GPU setup. For SWE-bench Lite evaluation, the approach is agentless (no iterative LLM calls per step), reducing per-task LLM cost compared to agent-loop approaches. No per-token API cost if running fully locally.
code_artifact_linkage: symbol (function/class) — primary. AST-node (ATTRIBUTE level) — partial. file-path — via PACKAGE/FILE hierarchy. No line-level granularity (unlike RepoGraph). No issue-PR-to-symbol chain (unlike KGCompass). The 7-type node schema gives a well-structured symbol-level view: FUNCTION and CLASS nodes are first-class, with explicit CALLS and EXTENDS edges capturing inter-symbol relationships.
code_analysis_features: Call graph (first-party, via CALLS edges from semantic analysis). Dependency graph / import resolution (first-party, via IMPORTS edges). Inheritance graph (first-party, via EXTENDS and IMPLEMENTS edges). Symbol index (first-party — FUNCTION, CLASS, ATTRIBUTE nodes). No diff-awareness. No type system integration. Python-first via AST; multi-language support depends on parser availability but not documented for languages beyond Python in the paper.
memory_os_layer: parametric (partially) + plaintext. The Adapter and the graph-attention injection into the LLM's attention matrix constitute a lightweight parametric/activation layer — the code graph structure replaces the LLM's attention mask, making this the only system among the three that operates partially at the activation tier. The R4 RAG chain also operates at the plaintext tier. No cross-type migration documented.
self_improvement_mechanism: none — static trained model; the Adapter and fine-tuned LLM are fixed post-training. No online learning or feedback loop from repair outcomes.
provenance_standard: none — no audit trail; no provenance metadata on graph edges
benchmark_results: SWE-bench Lite: 43.00% resolution rate with Qwen2.5-72B (open-source model). Ranked 1st among open-weight model methods, 2nd among open-source system methods overall, 8th overall (all methods). Surpasses previous best open-source model-based method by +12.33 percentage points. NeurIPS 2025 accepted poster.
strengths: 1. Highest SWE-bench Lite score among open-weight model methods (43%), demonstrating that structured code graph injection into LLM attention directly improves repair quality without a complex agent loop. 2. Rich 7-node/5-edge code schema (REPO/PACKAGE/FILE/CLASS/FUNCTION/ATTRIBUTE with contains/calls/imports/extends/implements) is the most semantically complete code-structure schema among the three papers, and maps cleanly to the Symbol/File/Module sublayer of Gimle §5. 3. Agentless design with R4 pipeline reduces per-repair cost compared to multi-step agent frameworks while achieving competitive performance.
weaknesses: 1. Parametric architecture is incompatible with runtime RAG — injecting graph structure into LLM attention requires a specially trained/fine-tuned model (Qwen2.5-72B CGM variant); you cannot plug the graph into an arbitrary LLM at inference time. Gimle's design must use the RAG/graph-traversal path, not the attention-injection path. 2. No temporal model and no issue/PR entity type — the schema is code-structure-only; it cannot represent issues, decisions, or the evolution of code over time, all of which are central to Gimle §5. 3. GPU infrastructure requirement is heavy — Qwen2.5-72B inference needs significant compute; not suitable for lightweight local deployment.
fit_with_gimle_section_5: Verdict: steal-specific-ideas. Ideas to steal: (1) The 7-node code schema is the most directly adoptable asset. Gimle §5's File and Symbol entity types should incorporate CGM's hierarchy: `(:Repo)-[:CONTAINS]->(:Package)-[:CONTAINS]->(:File)-[:CONTAINS]->(:Class)-[:CONTAINS]->(:Function)-[:CONTAINS]->(:Attribute)`. The reference edges `CALLS`, `IMPORTS`, `EXTENDS`, `IMPLEMENTS` should be first-class edge types in Gimle's graph. Schema snippet: `(:Symbol {kind: 'function|class|attribute', name, file_path})-[:CALLS]->(:Symbol)`, `(:Symbol)-[:EXTENDS]->(:Symbol)`. (2) The R4 retrieval chain pattern (Rewriter→Retriever→Reranker→Reader) is instructive for Gimle's retrieval layer: query rewriting before graph traversal, then LLM-based reranking of candidate subgraphs, can be implemented using Gimle's existing LLM plumbing without requiring the attention-injection component. Incompatibilities: the attention-matrix injection (the CGM's core innovation) requires a custom-trained model and cannot be adopted without retraining; Gimle should treat this as inspiration for the schema richness needed, not as a deployable component. The absence of bi-temporal metadata and issue/PR nodes means the CGM schema must be extended rather than adopted whole.
